title: Core spinning roving around wire
subtitle: Making a flexible, poseable yarn for sculptural flowers, stems, and jewellery
excerpt: Core spinning around a wire armature produces a poseable yarn: the wire holds the shape while the wool wrapping gives it texture and colour. Used for making flowers, plant stems, and wired jewellery components. The technique requires careful tension management as the wheel winds on, and a secure finish at each end to prevent the wrapping from sliding along the wire.
sourceNotes: Wire core spinning for sculptural and jewellery applications is a contemporary craft technique documented across fibre arts workshops and maker publications. The tension management principles are shared with general core spinning practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A spinning technique in which a non-fibrous core material (a thread, yarn, wire, or cord) is held under tension while fibre is drafted and wrapped around it as the wheel turns. The core runs through the centre of the finished yarn and provides structure, while the outer fibre layer provides texture and colour.

A single strand of yarn spun from prepared fibre before any plying. In core spinning, the singles wraps around the core rather than twisting solely upon itself; the core carries most of the structural tension in the finished strand.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. The flyer turns fast; tie hair back and roll sleeves.

attrs.title: Wire handling
attrs.body: Cut wire ends are sharp. Bend all cut ends over immediately. Keep wire away from the drive band and flyer mechanism.

content.text: , the wheel's twist wraps fibre around a central core rather than building a conventional yarn structure. When that core is a fine wire, the result is a

that bends and holds a shape. Wired yarn made this way is useful for flower petals and leaves (where the finished piece needs to hold a curved shape), plant stem wrapping, and wired earring or brooch components where the yarn contributes texture and colour rather than structural support.

The technique is classed as advanced. The motion itself is simple, but the variables take a session or two to calibrate. Wire stiffness resists the wheel's take-up. Wrapping tension determines how tightly the wool covers the wire. Any unevenness in the wrapping is visible in the finished piece.

items.name: Fine florist or armature wire, 0.3-0.5 mm gauge
Prepared roving (merino or fine wool blend), 5 mm wide strips
Spinning wheel (Saxony or castle), set to a low to mid ratio

content.text: Step 1: Wrap the wire start. Cut a length of wire suited to your project (a standard stem length for flowers is 20-25 cm; earring components are typically 5-8 cm). Bend the starting end over by 5 mm and press it flat with round-nose pliers: this prevents the roving from sliding off the end when you begin. Do not thread the wire through the orifice of the wheel. The wire will be held in your non-dominant hand as the wheel wraps roving around it.

Step 2: Set the wheel to a low take-up. The wire does not wind onto the bobbin. It is held by hand throughout. Some spinners mount the wire on a bobbin-spool jig for longer lengths. For short project lengths (under 30 cm), hand-holding is simpler and more controllable.

Step 3: Attach the roving to the wire. Hold the starting end of the wire in your non-dominant hand. Treadle slowly and bring a 5 mm wide strip of roving up to the orifice area; let the wheel's twist enter the roving for a few centimetres to build a small twisted leader. Bring the wire close to the orifice, hold the twisted leader against the wire, and allow a few more treadle strokes to wrap the leader around the wire start. This is the attachment point.

Step 4: Maintain even tension and advance slowly. Hold the wire at a consistent distance from the orifice: approximately 10-12 cm. Feed the roving steadily as the wheel turns. The wrapping should cover the wire completely with no gaps. Move the wire forward (towards the orifice, then slowly back) to advance the wrapping along its length. If the wrapping feels loose, reduce the distance between wire and orifice slightly. If it feels very tight and the wool is pulling thin, increase the distance.

Step 5: Keep the roving strip width consistent. A 5 mm strip of merino roving gives approximately 2-3 mm of finished wrapped diameter on 0.3 mm wire. If you need a thicker result for flower petals or stems, use two strips fed simultaneously. Keep both strips parallel rather than twisting them together before they reach the orifice.

Step 6: Stop treadling 2-3 cm before the end of the wire. Use the scissors to cut the roving, leaving a 5-6 cm tail. Twist this tail by hand in the same direction as the wheel was turning, then wrap it tightly around the wire end two or three times and tuck the tip back under the wrapping. This can be secured further with a small dab of clear craft glue if the project will be handled frequently.
Step 7: Bend the wire tip over 5 mm with round-nose pliers immediately after finishing. This prevents the sharp cut end from snagging the wrapping or causing a scratch during handling.

items.problem: The roving wrapping slides along the wire rather than staying put
items.cause: The starting attachment was not secure enough, or the twist in the wrapping is too low to grip the smooth wire surface.
items.fix: Increase treadle speed slightly to add more twist to the wrapping. At the start, use a small loop of waste roving to lock the starting end before attaching the main strip. If slippage continues after finishing, a 2 cm strip of double-sided tape wrapped around the wire under the starting section gives the roving a surface to grip.

items.problem: Bare wire is visible through gaps in the wrapping
items.cause: The roving strip is too narrow or the advance rate along the wire is too fast, so successive wraps do not overlap.
items.fix: Slow the rate at which you move the wire, or widen the roving strip to 6-7 mm. A second pass over any bare sections after the first wrapping is complete is a reliable fix: the second layer will fill any gaps.

items.problem: The wheel's drive band catches on the wire
items.cause: The wire is being held too close to the orifice and has drifted towards the flyer.
items.fix: Move the wire back to at least 10 cm from the orifice and keep it in the centre of your body, not to one side. If the wheel makes a catching sound, stop immediately and check the flyer for wire contact.
